issues.display_title: Issue #66: Add "View Last Tour" Shortcut Button to Main Screen
issues.main_description_html:
Add "View Last Tour" Shortcut Button to Main Screen

Description

User feedback indicates a discoverability issue in the app's navigation flow. After shooting/creating a tour, users are confused about how to access it. They do not intuitively realize they need to open the hamburger menu and navigate to the specific "Google Tours" or "Real Estate Tours" sections.

To improve the user experience and reduce friction, we need to add a direct "View Last Tour" shortcut button on the main "360 Virtual Tour Maker" screen.

"We need to add View Last Tour, because people don't understand that once they've shot a tour, they need to go to the menu and go to Google Tours or Real Estate tours." — user feedback

Sub-task

  • Add a new button labeled "View Last Tour" below the existing "Create Google Business Tour" card (and above the Learn/Help section), as indicated in the mock-up.

Placement (per mock-up)

360 Virtual Tour Maker (main screen)
 ├─ What tour you would like to create?
 ├─ [ Create Real Estate Tour ]      (card + description)
 ├─ [ Create Google Business Tour ]  (card + description)
 ├─ [ View Last Tour ]   ← NEW shortcut button (here)
 └─ Learn How to Use GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker  (Learn/Help)

Behavior

  • Tapping View Last Tour opens the user's most recently created/edited tour directly (skipping the hamburger menu → Google Tours / Real Estate Tours path).
  • "Last tour" = the most recent tour regardless of type (Real Estate or Google Business).
  • If there is no tour yet, the button is hidden (or disabled with a hint) — define per design.

Acceptance Criteria

  • A "View Last Tour" button appears on the main screen, positioned below the Create Google Business Tour card and above the Learn/Help section (matches the mock-up).
  • Tapping it navigates directly to the most recently created/edited tour.
  • Empty state handled: no tours → button hidden or disabled with guidance.
  • Styling is on-brand and consistent with the existing cards/buttons (light + dark mode).
  • Label is sourced from i18n (localizable), not hardcoded.
  • Responsive across phone sizes; no layout overlap with adjacent cards.

Out of Scope

  • Reworking the hamburger menu / Google Tours / Real Estate Tours sections.
  • Tour list/detail screens themselves (only the shortcut entry point).

🧪 Test / Review Scenarios (for tester)

TC-1 — Button visible & placed correctly

  1. Open the main "360 Virtual Tour Maker" screen with at least one existing tour.
  2. Expected: "View Last Tour" button shows below "Create Google Business Tour" and above the Learn/Help link, matching the mock-up.

TC-2 — Opens the last tour (happy path)

  1. Create/shoot a tour, return to the main screen, tap View Last Tour.
  2. Expected: The just-created tour opens directly (no menu navigation needed).

TC-3 — "Last" reflects most recent

  1. Create Tour A, then Tour B; return to main → tap View Last Tour.
  2. Expected: Tour B (the most recent) opens.

TC-4 — Mixed tour types

  1. Make the most recent tour a Real Estate one, then later a Google Business one.
  2. Expected: The shortcut opens whichever is most recent, regardless of type.

TC-5 — Empty state (no tours)

  1. Fresh account / no tours created.
  2. Expected: Button is hidden or disabled with guidance (per design); no crash or dead tap.

TC-6 — Theme & responsiveness

  1. View in light and dark mode on small and large phones.
  2. Expected: On-brand styling; no clipping/overlap with adjacent cards.

TC-7 — Localization

  1. Switch app language.
  2. Expected: Button label is translated; no raw i18n key.

Platforms: Android + iOS
Reference: See attached mock-up (View Last Tour positioned below the Create Google Business Tour card).

RESULT

Redesign — Delete Account (Flutter UI Design)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter — UI/UX design.
Source reference: RN account deletion flow with OTP confirmation (src/pages/setting/delete-account, .../otp); see docs/analyze.md §2.8 (account management — account deletion with OTP).

Goal

Redesign the Delete Account flow so it is clear, safe, and on-brand: the user understands the consequences, confirms intent, verifies via OTP, and receives unambiguous success/failure feedback.

Scope

  • Visual design for the full delete-account journey:
    1. Entry point (in Settings) with a clear warning about permanence/consequences.
    2. Confirmation step (explicit intent, e.g. type/confirm).
    3. OTP verification screen (request code, enter code, resend, error states).
    4. Result states: success (account deleted / signed out) and failure (wrong/expired OTP, network error).
  • Light + dark mode; responsive across phone sizes; on-brand (GoThru) styling.
  • Deliverables: mockups/specs for each screen + state, handoff-ready for Flutter.

Out of Scope

  • Implementing the deletion logic / OTP backend (engineering task).
  • Other Settings screens not part of the delete flow.

Acceptance Criteria

  • Mockups cover every step: entry/warning → confirm → OTP → success/failure.
  • Consequences of deletion are communicated clearly before the destructive action.
  • OTP screen covers request, entry, resend, and error/expired states.
  • Destructive action styled as such (clear, not accidental); cancel/back paths present.
  • Light + dark variants; responsive on small/large phones.
  • Labels localizable (i18n); on-brand and consistent with the design system.

🧪 Review Scenarios (for design QA)

  1. Flow completeness — every step (entry → confirm → OTP → result) has a defined screen and state; no dead ends.
  2. Clarity of consequences — a user clearly understands deletion is permanent before proceeding.
  3. OTP states — request, enter, resend, wrong code, expired code, and success are all designed.
  4. Destructive affordance — the delete action is visually distinct (e.g. danger styling) and not easy to trigger by accident; cancel is always available.
  5. Theme & contrast — light/dark variants legible; danger colors meet contrast.
  6. Responsiveness — layouts hold on small/large phones without clipping.
  7. Brand consistency — typography, color, components match the Street Editor Flutter design system.

Platforms: Android + iOS

RESULT

Redesign — Street View Account (Flutter UI Design)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter — UI/UX design.
Source reference: RN Google account / Street View publishing integration (src/pages/setting/google-account; Google sign-in tokens google_token/google_access_token); see docs/analyze.md §2.7 (publish), §3.4 (Google integration), §2.8 (account management).

Goal

Redesign the Street View Account screen so the user can connect/manage the Google (Street View / Business Profile) account used to publish tours, with clear connected/disconnected states — modernized and on-brand for the Flutter app.

Scope

  • Redesign the Street View account management screen across its states:
    1. Not connected state — explain why connecting is needed + "Connect Google / Street View account" action.
    2. Connecting — in-progress / Google auth handoff.
    3. Connected — show the linked account (name/email/avatar) + disconnect action.
    4. Error/permission states (auth denied, network error, insufficient permissions).
  • Disconnect confirmation; what happens to publishing when disconnected (messaging).
  • Light + dark mode; responsive; on-brand (GoThru) styling.
  • Deliverables: redesigned mockups/specs per state, handoff-ready for Flutter.

Out of Scope

  • Implementing Google OAuth / Street View publish APIs (engineering task).
  • The publish-to-Google action itself (separate flow).

Acceptance Criteria

  • Redesigned mockups cover not-connected, connecting, connected, and error/permission states.
  • Connected state clearly shows which Google/Street View account is linked.
  • Connect and Disconnect actions are clear; disconnect asks for confirmation.
  • Messaging explains the impact on publishing when not connected.
  • Light + dark variants; responsive on small/large phones.
  • Labels localizable (i18n); on-brand and consistent with the design system.

🧪 Review Scenarios (for design QA)

  1. State coverage — not-connected, connecting, connected, error/permission states all redesigned.
  2. Connected clarity — the linked account identity (name/email/avatar) is obvious.
  3. Connect / disconnect — both actions are clear; disconnect has a confirmation and explains consequences for publishing.
  4. Error handling — auth-denied / network / insufficient-permission states have defined screens + guidance.
  5. Theme & responsiveness — light/dark legible; layouts hold on small/large phones.
  6. Brand consistency — matches the Street Editor Flutter design system (type, color, components).

Platforms: Android + iOS

RESULT
